one_line_takeaway: MCGMark embeds a 24-bit, code-structure-aware watermark during LLM code generation (not post-hoc) to trace which user prompted an LLM to produce a given (possibly malicious) code snippet, reaching ~85% embedding success, 97.8% detection success, and near-chance (47.8%) human distinguishability, while outperforming WLLM/MPAC/PostMark baselines on code-quality preservation (CodeBLEU) and tamper robustness (1147/1200 vs 971-1110/1200 attacks survived) -- but the scheme only attributes generation, it does not detect or prevent malicious code generation itself.
key_finding: MCGMark achieved an 85.2% average watermark embedding success rate across the three LLMs (88.9% DeepSeek-Coder, 87.2% StarCoder2, 79.6% CodeLlama) at a 400-token cap for a 24-bit watermark; relaxing the token cap to 2048 for the 25/406 DeepSeek-Coder failures attributable to short/structurally-restrictive code recovered 21/25, projecting an overall embedding rate of ~94.1%. Watermark detection success rate on DeepSeek-Coder was 97.8% (353/361 successfully-embedded tasks), beating baselines MPAC (87.5%), WLLM (84.2%), and PostMark (21.4%) on the reported Table 3 detect-rate column (note: MCGMark's own detect-rate figure of 86.9% appears in Table 3 alongside the 97.8% figure computed differently in the text -- these are two separate detection metrics reported in the paper, not a contradiction to be resolved by this review). CodeBLEU (higher = less quality degradation) favored MCGMark at 0.27 versus WLLM 0.19, PostMark 0.16, MPAC 0.21. The human user study found participants could distinguish watermarked from unwatermarked code at only 47.8% accuracy (near chance, 500 pairs/1000 judgments, 95% CI [21.972,27.828] on raw correct-counts), supporting an imperceptibility claim. Robustness: across 1,200 attacks per method (4,800 total), MCGMark survived 1147/1200 (highest of the four), versus MPAC 1062/1200, WLLM 1110/1200, PostMark 971/1200; MCGMark achieved complete (150/150) defense on attacks 3,6,7,8 but degraded to 79.3% (119/150) on Attack 5 (modify/remove assignment statements), attributed to token-matching/regex limitations and the 400-token cap truncating multi-round embedding. Hash-value ablation showed fixed hash keys yielded only 18-44% embedding success versus MCGMark's dynamic pseudo-random hashing, which the paper credits with the reported 85%+ rates. Time overhead: MCGMark added roughly 7-10x latency over unwatermarked generation (e.g., DeepSeek-Coder 14.04s -> 103.48s), comparable to MPAC (114.45s total) and cheaper than PostMark (202.69s total), with WLLM cheapest (42.91s total).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is twofold: (1) MCGTest, a 406-prompt dataset for eliciting LLM-generated malicious code, built from an empirical study collecting 3,644 web/literature results and 21,959 GitHub malicious-code repositories, filtered down via a two-group manual pipeline to 524 malicious code instances and then 406 distinct malicious-code-functionality prompts; and (2) MCGMark, an online (during-generation, not post-hoc) multi-bit watermarking scheme for tracing which user/identity caused an LLM to generate a given piece of (malicious) code. MCGMark works by: partitioning the LLM vocabulary into two sets via a pseudo-random hash and steering token selection toward one set or the other to encode watermark bits; an outlier-management step (Algorithm 1, IQR-based) that folds high-probability 'outlier' tokens into the selected vocabulary set to preserve code quality, with an error-correction bit recording when this occurred; and a code-structure-aware watermark-skipping mechanism (Algorithm 2, seven patterns) that avoids embedding watermark bits in code elements easily altered by attackers (identifiers, comments, output statements, numbers, blank lines, assignments/comparisons), determined empirically from Section 3's code-structure, code-clone, and malicious-code-instance analysis (summarized in Table 1). Detection is lightweight: it needs only the tokenizer and vocabulary/hash (no LLM inference), tokenizes the code, strips skip-eligible elements via the same Algorithm 2, and recovers the user ID via Detection Bits XOR Error-Correction Bits across multiple round-robin-embedded watermark segments.
threat_model: Adversary: a malicious developer who prompts an SSP-hosted Code LLM (e.g., DeepSeek-Coder, StarCoder2, CodeLlama) to generate malicious code (malware, spyware, ransomware components, browser-history stealers, etc.) and who may subsequently try to strip an embedded watermark by editing the generated code (renaming identifiers, altering/removing comments, changing literals, altering assignment/comparison statements, or adding comments/assignments/redundant statements) without breaking functionality. Defender: the SSP, which wants to embed an imperceptible, per-user identity watermark into all code it generates (benign and malicious alike -- the paper explicitly does not attempt malicious/benign classification) so that if malicious code surfaces later, the SSP can trace it back to the requesting user/account without needing to re-run the LLM or store external logs. This is a traceability/attribution threat model, not a jailbreak-prevention or malicious-code-detection threat model -- the paper states outright that MCGMark 'cannot eliminate the malicious nature of generated code or prevent its generation.'
limitations: The paper's own Section 6 lists three limitations: (1) Defense against specific attacks is incomplete -- failures stem from a generic regex-based token-matching approach that cannot fully handle the >3.2K-token, non-human-grammar-following LLM vocabularies, causing degraded robustness against Attack 5 (assignment modification, 79.3% success) and related failures on Attacks 1/2/4; inserting non-watermarked human-written code can also contaminate detection, though the paper argues this typically changes code functionality. (2) Watermark embedding inevitably degrades code quality somewhat (via outlier token selection and error-correction-bit embedding), though this can be tuned via the outlier-filtering strictness parameter, trading randomness/quality for watermark reliability. (3) MCGMark introduces unavoidable extra time overhead common to all watermarking schemes, though the paper argues it remains comparable to baselines and does not require loading extra models/databases at detection time. Additional constraints evident from the text but not framed as 'Limitations': the scheme applies watermarking to ALL generated code, not just malicious code, since MCGMark performs no malicious/benign classification; it is restricted to Python only, due to code-structure differences across languages; it requires open-source/white-box access to model vocabulary and token probabilities during generation, so it cannot be retrofitted onto already-deployed closed models without SSP cooperation; and detection accuracy is degraded by tokenizer-induced token-splitting discrepancies (8 of 361 successfully-embedded cases failed detection for this reason).

datasets_used: MCGTest (paper's own): 406 malicious-code-generation prompts, built from 524 filtered malicious code instances (129 from empirical/literature collection, 395 from GitHub malicious-code repositories), derived from 3,644 raw web/literature results and 21,959 GitHub repositories
CodeBLEU evaluation framework (Ren et al., cited [73]) used to score code-generation quality before/after watermarking
quantitative_results: Watermark embedding success rate: 85.2% average across 3 LLMs (DeepSeek-Coder-6.7b-instruct 88.9%, StarCoder2-7b-hf 87.2%, CodeLlama-7b-hf 79.6%), 24-bit watermark, 400-token cap
Extended-token retest: 21/25 previously-failed DeepSeek-Coder tasks embedded successfully at 2048-token cap; projected overall embedding rate ~94.1%
Watermark detection success rate on DeepSeek-Coder: 97.8% (353/361 successfully embedded tasks); Table 3 also reports a 86.9% detect-rate figure for MCGMark vs WLLM 84.2%, MPAC 87.5%, PostMark 21.4%
CodeBLEU (quality-preservation) scores: MCGMark 0.27, MPAC 0.21, WLLM 0.19, PostMark 0.16
User study: 10 developers, 1000 judgments over 500 watermarked/unwatermarked code pairs -> 47.8% average accuracy distinguishing watermarked code (near-chance)
Robustness: MCGMark survived 1147/1200 attacks; WLLM 1110/1200; MPAC 1062/1200; PostMark 971/1200 (4,800 attacks total across 4 methods, 8 attack types x 150 attacks each)

MCGMark per-attack-type defense: complete (150/150) on Attacks 3,6,7,8; 94% (141/150, i.e. 9 failures) on Attack 1; 93.3% on Attack 2; 91.3% (13 failures/150) on Attack 4; 79.3% (119/150, 31 failures) on Attack 5
Hash-value ablation: fixed hash keys 7,775 / 666 / 15,485,865 / 2 -> embedding success rates of 18%, 38%, 36%, 44% respectively, vs MCGMark's dynamic hashing achieving ~85%
Time overhead (with vs without watermark, seconds, averaged over 406 prompts): DeepSeek-Coder 14.04->103.48 (7.37x), StarCoder2 11.69->120.55 (10.31x), CodeLlama 9.65->91.31 (9.87x)
Time overhead by method (embed/detect/total, seconds): no-watermark baseline 14.04 total; WLLM 41.62/1.29/42.91; PostMark 202.48/0.21/202.69; MPAC 112.31/2.14/114.45; MCGMark 99.12/4.36/103.48
Without error-correction bits, theoretical successful-detection probability for the 24-bit watermark is only 0.19%, motivating the error-correction-bit design
baselines_compared: WLLM (Kirchenbauer et al. red/green-list zero-bit LLM text watermark, gamma=0.25, delta=2)
MPAC (multi-bit extension of WLLM via vocabulary partitioning)
